metadata.usage: Select one protocol_profile + one runtime_profile. Merge their parameters to form a complete APOS simulation input. Use Frequency and Ca from environment_constants.
metadata.accuracy: ±30-40% engineering estimates. k_ser and k_net now absorb full runtime overhead. Relative ordering between entries is more reliable than absolute values.
metadata.composition_rule: Final config = protocol_profile fields + runtime_profile fields + environment_constants fields.

environment_constants.description: Shared system environment. Override per deployment target.

Frequency.note: Baseline mid-range server CPU (3 GHz). Adjust for target hardware.

Ca.note: Coefficient of variation for inter-arrival times. 1.0 = Poisson arrivals (standard assumption). Increase for bursty traffic.

notes.H: Typical HTTP/1.1 headers: method, host, content-type, authorization, etc. Range: 300-800 bytes.
notes.O_net: TCP 3-way handshake + TLS 1.3 negotiation. Range: 1000-2000 bytes.
notes.O_cpu: TLS handshake CPU cost (ECDHE key exchange). Range: 100k-300k cycles.
notes.Cs: REST latency variability is moderate-high due to JSON parsing variance and GC pauses.

notes.H: HTTP/2 HPACK compressed headers. After dynamic table warm-up, headers compress to 20-80 bytes.
notes.O_net: TCP + TLS + HTTP/2 SETTINGS + WINDOW_UPDATE frames. Slightly heavier than HTTP/1.1.
notes.O_cpu: TLS + HTTP/2 HPACK dynamic table initialization overhead.
notes.Cs: Slightly lower variability than HTTP/1.1 due to multiplexing smoothing out latency spikes.

notes.H: gRPC framing: 5-byte gRPC header + HTTP/2 frame header (~9 bytes). Range: 15-60 bytes with metadata.
notes.O_net: Same as REST/HTTP2 — shared TLS + HTTP/2 handshake cost.
notes.O_cpu: TLS + HTTP/2 HPACK table init + Protobuf schema registration. Higher than REST due to additional setup.
notes.Cs: Protobuf parsing is highly deterministic. Low service time variance.

notes.H: Per-message overhead within an established stream is minimal — only gRPC 5-byte frame header.
notes.O_net: Same connection establishment as gRPC unary.
notes.O_cpu: Slightly higher setup due to stream lifecycle management.
notes.Cs: Very low variance — steady stream of uniform messages, no per-request connection cost.

notes.H: WebSocket frame header: 2-10 bytes. Extremely low per-message overhead after handshake.
notes.O_net: HTTP Upgrade handshake + TLS. Slightly heavier than plain HTTP due to Upgrade negotiation.
notes.O_cpu: TLS + HTTP Upgrade + WebSocket handshake CPU cost.
notes.Cs: Lower variance than REST — persistent connection eliminates per-request setup jitter.

notes.H: Kafka record header: topic, partition, offset, timestamp, key metadata. Range: 30-100 bytes.
notes.O_net: TCP + Kafka API handshake + metadata fetch. Heavier than HTTP due to broker metadata protocol.
notes.O_cpu: Broker metadata fetch + partition assignment + optional compression init (Snappy/LZ4).
notes.Cs: Batching smooths out latency variance significantly. Low Cs for batch-heavy producers.
notes.model_note: R represents messages/s not requests/s. Batching means effective O_cpu is amortized across batch size — divide O_cpu by average batch size for high-throughput scenarios.

notes.O_net: TCP + broker handshake + consumer group coordination.
notes.O_cpu: Offset management + consumer group heartbeat + fetch request processing.
notes.Cs: Poll-based consumption is highly regular. Very low variance.
notes.model_note: R represents messages/s consumed. Consumer group rebalancing events create latency spikes not captured by steady-state Cs.

notes.H: AMQP frame header + routing metadata (exchange, routing key, properties). Range: 60-200 bytes.
notes.O_net: TCP + TLS + AMQP connection handshake (START, SECURE, TUNE, OPEN). Heaviest in this table.
notes.O_cpu: AMQP connection negotiation is expensive — multiple round-trips with capability exchange.
notes.Cs: Higher variance due to broker-side routing, queue inspection, and optional persistence writes.

notes.H: MQTT fixed header: 2 bytes minimum. Variable header + topic: typically 5-20 bytes total.
notes.O_net: TCP + optional TLS + MQTT CONNECT/CONNACK. Lightest connection overhead in this table.
notes.O_cpu: Minimal broker-side processing. Optional TLS dominates if enabled.
notes.Cs: QoS 0 (fire-and-forget) has low variance. QoS 1-2 adds acknowledgment round-trips increasing Cs.

notes.H: NATS text protocol: PUB <subject> <size>\r\n. Minimal framing.
notes.O_net: TCP + NATS INFO/CONNECT handshake. Extremely lightweight — designed for low-overhead messaging.
notes.O_cpu: Near-zero broker overhead. NATS is optimized for minimal processing per message.
notes.Cs: Very consistent latency. NATS is purpose-built for predictable low-latency delivery.

notes.H: UDP header is fixed at 8 bytes. No framing overhead beyond this.
notes.O_net: No connection establishment. Zero handshake cost.
notes.Cs: High variance — no delivery guarantee, no congestion control, packet loss and reordering cause significant latency spikes.
notes.model_note: Kingman's formula assumes reliable delivery. UDP packet loss is not modeled by APOS. Use with caution for lossy networks.

notes.H: QUIC short header: 1-byte first byte + connection ID (8 bytes) + packet number. Range: 15-25 bytes.
notes.O_net: QUIC 0-RTT or 1-RTT handshake. Faster than TCP+TLS due to combined crypto+transport handshake.
notes.O_cpu: TLS 1.3 integrated into QUIC handshake. Similar to TCP+TLS but fewer round trips.
notes.Cs: Better than TCP under packet loss due to independent stream delivery. Lower head-of-line blocking.

notes.B_base: ~8 MB. Minimal runtime footprint. No VM, no GC, no module cache. OS + stack + static buffers only.
notes.k_ser: Fastest JSON parser (simdjson). Near-zero overhead for binary/protobuf.
notes.k_net: Minimal syscall overhead. Epoll-based event loop with zero-copy where possible.
notes.sigma: Low friction despite multi-threading due to lock-free designs.
notes.tau: No GC. Manual memory management means tau=1.0 (baseline).

notes.B_base: ~10 MB. Tokio runtime + Actix worker threads + static allocations. No GC overhead.
notes.k_ser: serde_json is fast but slightly behind simdjson. Prost for Protobuf is highly optimized.
notes.k_net: Tokio async runtime adds minimal overhead over raw epoll.
notes.sigma: Async multi-threaded. Work-stealing scheduler has low contention.
notes.tau: No GC. Ownership model eliminates memory pressure coefficient.

notes.B_base: ~25 MB. Go runtime + goroutine scheduler + GC metadata + stdlib buffers. Measured at idle.
notes.k_ser: Revised upward from v2. encoding/json reflection cost + goroutine stack allocation per request absorbed here.
notes.k_net: Revised upward. Goroutine-per-connection scheduling cost fully absorbed.
notes.sigma: Go scheduler (GMP model) is efficient. Low sigma despite goroutine overhead.
notes.tau: Low-pause GC (concurrent mark-and-sweep). Minor pressure from goroutine stack allocations.

notes.B_base: ~20 MB. Slightly lower than net/http due to fasthttp object reuse reducing steady-state heap.
notes.k_ser: fasthttp byte-slice reuse and bytedance/sonic reduce allocation overhead significantly.
notes.k_net: fasthttp connection object pooling reduces per-request syscall and allocation cost.
notes.tau: Same GC as net/http but fewer allocations per request in practice.

notes.B_base: ~120 MB. JVM baseline heap (metaspace + code cache + JIT compiled classes) before handling requests.
notes.k_ser: Jackson JSON object allocation cost fully absorbed. Protobuf via protoc-gen-java is well-optimized.
notes.k_net: NIO event loop efficient but JVM JNI boundary crossings add overhead vs native.
notes.sigma: Netty uses async event loop (Boss/Worker thread model). Low contention.
notes.tau: JVM object allocation per request is high. G1GC/ZGC manage it well but pause risk exists.

notes.B_base: ~180 MB. JVM baseline + Spring context (bean definitions, AOP proxies, autoconfiguration) loaded at startup.
notes.k_ser: Spring MVC reflection + interceptor chain + Jackson fully absorbed. Significantly higher than bare Netty.
notes.k_net: Thread-per-request Tomcat model. Context-switching cost fully absorbed into k_net.
notes.sigma: Thread-per-request is the dominant driver of high sigma. Spring WebFlux (Netty) would drop this to 0.03.
notes.tau: More objects allocated per request than Netty due to Spring abstractions.

notes.B_base: ~120 MB. V8 heap baseline + JIT compiled code + libuv handles + Node.js module cache at startup.
notes.k_ser: Revised upward from v2. V8 JSON.parse object creation + GC churn cost fully absorbed. Produces realistic ~15-25% CPU at 10k RPS.
notes.k_net: Revised upward. libuv event loop JS/C++ boundary cost + V8 callback overhead fully absorbed.
notes.sigma: Single-threaded event loop means near-zero context switching. Lowest sigma in this table.
notes.tau: V8 GC (Scavenger + Mark-Compact) creates pressure under high object churn from JSON parsing.

notes.B_base: ~130 MB. Slightly higher than native http due to Fastify schema compilation and plugin system at startup.
notes.k_ser: fast-json-stringify schema-based serialization reduces runtime cost vs JSON.parse reflection.
notes.k_net: Leaner middleware pipeline than Express. Router overhead lower than native http per request.
notes.sigma: Same single-threaded event loop as native Node.js.
notes.tau: Fewer allocations per request due to schema-based serialization reducing GC pressure.

notes.B_base: ~80 MB. .NET runtime + JIT compiled assemblies + Kestrel connection pool. Lower than JVM due to AOT-friendly design.
notes.k_ser: System.Text.Json Span<T>-based parsing reduces allocation cost. Revised upward slightly from v2 to absorb middleware cost.
notes.k_net: Kestrel async I/O with minimal overhead. Revised upward to absorb full pipeline cost.
notes.sigma: Async/await model with thread pool. Low contention, efficient scheduler.
notes.tau: Generational GC with server GC mode. Value types and Span<T> reduce allocation pressure vs JVM.

notes.B_base: ~50 MB. CPython interpreter + asyncio event loop + imported modules (fastapi, starlette, pydantic etc.).
notes.k_ser: Revised upward. CPython interpreter overhead fully absorbed. orjson would reduce to ~8-10.
notes.k_net: Revised upward. asyncio coroutine dispatch + Python/C extension boundary cost absorbed.
notes.tau: Reference counting + cyclic GC creates high memory pressure under object-heavy workloads.

notes.B_base: ~200 MB. Per-process isolation means no memory sharing. Each worker forks with full interpreter + app state. Assumes 4 workers; divide by worker count for per-worker estimate.
notes.k_ser: Revised upward. WSGI middleware stack + CPython overhead fully absorbed.
notes.k_net: Blocking I/O per worker. Full process context cost absorbed.
notes.sigma: Process-per-request (fork model). Highest sigma in this table due to OS process scheduling.
notes.tau: Same GC as uvicorn. Per-process isolation prevents any memory sharing between requests.

notes.B_base: ~150 MB. MRI Ruby interpreter + Rails/Sinatra framework loaded + GC metadata. Rails adds ~100 MB alone.
notes.k_ser: MRI Ruby JSON parsing is among the slowest due to GIL and interpreter overhead. Full cost absorbed.
notes.k_net: Thread-per-request with GIL limiting true parallelism. Full scheduling cost absorbed.
notes.sigma: Thread-per-request model. GIL reduces true parallelism but thread scheduling overhead remains.
notes.tau: Mark-and-sweep GC with highest object allocation rate in this table.
